title: SK Hynix targets August Nasdaq debut on the back of its Nvidia HBM lock
subtitle: The Korean memory giant is closing in on SEC approval of its ADR listing, with an HBM monopoly on Nvidia's Vera-Rubin platform behind the pitch.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: SK Hynix is on track for a Nasdaq debut as early as August, with the US Securities and Exchange Commission expected to clear its American Depositary Receipt application during the week of June 22. The deal could raise up to $14 billion. The chipmaker filed confidentially in March, picked Nasdaq over the NYSE to signal a tech-rather-than-industrial identity, and now sits on top of a multi-year memory pact with Nvidia announced June 7 that locks it in as the principal HBM supplier for the Vera-Rubin AI accelerator, Vera CPUs, the RTX Spark PC line, and the Jetson Thor robotics platform.

High-bandwidth memory is the part of the AI stack most people building models never see and never think about. Every frontier training run is bottlenecked on it; every accelerator generation needs more of it; and there are exactly three companies that make it at scale, with SK Hynix the runaway leader in HBM3E and HBM4 yields. The stock has roughly tripled in 2026, market cap pushed past a trillion dollars, and Wall Street banks have been tightening leverage terms to manage exposure — all signals that the float is being treated less like a chip company and more like an AI infrastructure proxy.

Two threads worth tracking. First, this is the first major Korean tech listing in the US in a generation, and Seoul is leaning in — Naver's gigawatt AI factory plan with Nvidia announced last week is the same playbook on the cloud side. Second, an SK Hynix that funds itself out of US capital markets at AI multiples can outspend Samsung and Micron on R&D, which compresses the HBM oligopoly into something closer to a duopoly. The Vera-Rubin generation will be the first one delivered under that structure.

Takeaway for learners: when you read about a new frontier model, the chokepoint upstream isn't the GPU — it's the memory bolted to the GPU. The companies that win the AI hardware decade are the ones with HBM yield, not the ones with the loudest model launches. Watch the SK Hynix S-1 when it becomes public; the gross-margin lines tell you more about where the value in this stack is settling than any model benchmark.
